title: CDMGRestrictions
tex_block_html: With the notations not-cdmg the restrictions in definition def-cdmg mean that the nodes $j \in J$ will not have any arrowheads pointing towards them: $ j \hus v \notin G$. Nodes $j \in J$ can only point towards nodes $v \in V$: edges $j \tuh v$ are allowed. Furthermore, no two nodes in $J$ are adjacent.

tex_statement.raw:
% Claim statement file for row claim_3_1 -- "CDMGRestrictions".
% Auto-generated stub by scaffold/solve_chapter.py at row start. The
% manager agent (or a worker it dispatches) fills the body below with the
% claim's statement(s) from the lecture notes -- statement only, no proof
% (proofs live in the sibling `claim_3_1_proof_*.tex` file).
%
% This file is a sibling of `main.tex` inside `<subsection>/tex/`. The
% `subfiles` package lets it render both standalone and as part of
% `main.tex`. Note: `main.tex` does NOT \subfile this statement file -- the
% sibling `_proof_` file restates the statement above the proof, so
% including both in `main.tex` would be redundant. This file exists for
% standalone reading / grep convenience.

\documentclass[main]{subfiles}

\begin{document}
% ref: claim_3_1 (statement)
% title: CDMGRestrictions
\def\rowref{claim\_3\_1}
\phantomsection\label{claim_3_1}

\begin{Rem}[CDMGRestrictions]
    Let $G = (J, V, E, L)$ be a CDMG (def \ref{def-cdmg}); we use the notation of \ref{not-cdmg} throughout. Recall, per def \ref{def-cdmg}, that $E \subseteq (J \cup V) \times V$, that $L \subseteq V \times V$ with $L$ symmetric and irreflexive, and that $J \cap V = \emptyset$.

    The restrictions encoded in def \ref{def-cdmg} have the following three consequences. In the LN wording, the clauses ``$j \hus v \notin G$'' and ``edges $j \tuh v$ are allowed'' carry an implicit universal quantification over the second-position variable $v$; we surface these quantifiers explicitly below, with $v$ ranging over $J \cup V$ in the first clause and over $V$ only in the second (see also the addition spelled out under sub-claim ii.).

    \begin{enumerate}[label=\roman*.)]
        \item \emph{No edge with an arrowhead at a $J$-node.}
              For every $j \in J$ and every $v \in J \cup V$,
              \[ j \hus v \notin G. \]
              Unfolding the shorthand $\hus$ via def \ref{not-cdmg} item~6 (so $j \hus v \in G$ abbreviates $(v, j) \in E \lor (j, v) \in L$), this is equivalent to the set-theoretic conjunction
              \[ (v, j) \notin E \;\land\; (j, v) \notin L. \]

        \item \emph{The type of $G$ does not forbid a directed edge from a $J$-node into a $V$-node.}
              For every $j \in J$ and every $v \in V$, the ordered pair $(j, v)$ satisfies $j \in J \cup V$ and $v \in V$, so the typing constraint $E \subseteq (J \cup V) \times V$ of def \ref{def-cdmg} is compatible with $(j, v) \in E$; equivalently, no restriction in def \ref{def-cdmg} forbids the inclusion of $(j, v)$ in $E$ (in the notation of \ref{not-cdmg}: no restriction forbids $j \tuh v \in G$). This is a \emph{permission} statement, not an existence statement: $(j, v) \in E$ is admissible by the definition for every $(j, v) \in J \times V$, but is not asserted to hold.

        \item \emph{No two $J$-nodes are adjacent.}
              For every $j_1 \in J$ and every $j_2 \in J$, the nodes $j_1$ and $j_2$ are \emph{not} adjacent in $G$ in the sense of def \ref{def-edge-relations} item~i.; equivalently,
              \[ (j_1, j_2) \notin E \;\land\; (j_2, j_1) \notin E \;\land\; (j_1, j_2) \notin L. \]
    \end{enumerate}
\end{Rem}

\end{document}
tex_statement.html:
Let $G = (J, V, E, L)$ be a CDMG (def def-cdmg); we use the notation of not-cdmg throughout. Recall, per def def-cdmg, that $E \subseteq (J \cup V) \times V$, that $L \subseteq V \times V$ with $L$ symmetric and irreflexive, and that $J \cap V = \emptyset$.

The restrictions encoded in def def-cdmg have the following three consequences. In the LN wording, the clauses “$j \hus v \notin G$” and “edges $j \tuh v$ are allowed” carry an implicit universal quantification over the second-position variable $v$; we surface these quantifiers explicitly below, with $v$ ranging over $J \cup V$ in the first clause and over $V$ only in the second (see also the addition spelled out under sub-claim ii.).

  1. No edge with an arrowhead at a $J$-node. For every $j \in J$ and every $v \in J \cup V$, \[ j \hus v \notin G. \] Unfolding the shorthand $\hus$ via def not-cdmg item 6 (so $j \hus v \in G$ abbreviates $(v, j) \in E \lor (j, v) \in L$), this is equivalent to the set-theoretic conjunction \[ (v, j) \notin E \;\land\; (j, v) \notin L. \]
  2. The type of $G$ does not forbid a directed edge from a $J$-node into a $V$-node. For every $j \in J$ and every $v \in V$, the ordered pair $(j, v)$ satisfies $j \in J \cup V$ and $v \in V$, so the typing constraint $E \subseteq (J \cup V) \times V$ of def def-cdmg is compatible with $(j, v) \in E$; equivalently, no restriction in def def-cdmg forbids the inclusion of $(j, v)$ in $E$ (in the notation of not-cdmg: no restriction forbids $j \tuh v \in G$). This is a permission statement, not an existence statement: $(j, v) \in E$ is admissible by the definition for every $(j, v) \in J \times V$, but is not asserted to hold.
  3. No two $J$-nodes are adjacent. For every $j_1 \in J$ and every $j_2 \in J$, the nodes $j_1$ and $j_2$ are not adjacent in $G$ in the sense of def def-edge-relations item i.; equivalently, \[ (j_1, j_2) \notin E \;\land\; (j_2, j_1) \notin E \;\land\; (j_1, j_2) \notin L. \]

tex_proof.raw:
% Claim proof file for row claim_3_1 -- "CDMGRestrictions" -- REFACTOR TWIN.
% This is the `cdmg_typed_edges`-refactor twin of
% `claim_3_1_proof_CDMGRestrictions.tex`, written alongside the original
% (which stays untouched until Phase 7 cleanup, when this twin is renamed
% over it). The only adaptation versus the original is the L-typing
% language: in the refactored `def_3_1`, the bidirected-edge carrier
% `L \subseteq V \times V` (with an explicit `hL_symm` axiom and an
% ordered-pair irreflexivity hypothesis) is replaced by
% `L \subseteq (V \times V) / {\sim}` -- the quotient of `V \times V` by
% the swap relation `(v_1, v_2) \sim (v_2, v_1)`, equivalently the set of
% unordered pairs of elements of `V`. Symmetry is now definitional under
% the quotient typing, and irreflexivity is phrased as "no diagonal class
% `s(v, v)` lies in `L`". The mathematical content of the remark and the
% structure of the proof (three sub-claims, case-split on the disjunction
% in each, contradict via `Disjoint J V`) are unchanged.
%
% This file is a sibling of `main.tex` inside `<subsection>/tex/`. The
% `subfiles` package lets it render both standalone and as part of
% `main.tex` via `\subfile{...}`.

\documentclass[main]{subfiles}

\begin{document}
% ref: claim_3_1 (proof, with statement restated for readability)
% title: CDMGRestrictions
\def\rowref{claim\_3\_1}
\phantomsection\label{claim_3_1}

% --- statement (restated) ---------------------------------------------------
\begin{Rem}[CDMGRestrictions]
    Let $G = (J, V, E, L)$ be a CDMG (def \ref{def-cdmg}); we use the notation of \ref{not-cdmg} throughout. Recall, per def \ref{def-cdmg}, that $E \subseteq (J \cup V) \times V$, and that $L \subseteq (V \times V) / {\sim}$ is a finite set of \emph{unordered} pairs of elements of $V$, where $\sim$ is the swap relation $(v_1, v_2) \sim (v_2, v_1)$ on $V \times V$. We write $s(a, b)$ for the equivalence class of the ordered pair $(a, b)$ under $\sim$; i.e.\ $s(a, b)$ is the unordered pair $\{a, b\}$ regarded as an element of the quotient $(V \times V) / {\sim}$, and in particular $s(a, b) = s(b, a)$ holds by construction. $L$ is required to be irreflexive in the sense that no diagonal class lies in $L$: $s(v, v) \notin L$ for every $v$. No separate symmetry axiom on $L$ is needed -- symmetry is built into the quotient typing. Finally, $J \cap V = \emptyset$.

    The restrictions encoded in def \ref{def-cdmg} have the following three consequences. In the LN wording, the clauses ``$j \hus v \notin G$'' and ``edges $j \tuh v$ are allowed'' carry an implicit universal quantification over the second-position variable $v$; we surface these quantifiers explicitly below, with $v$ ranging over $J \cup V$ in the first clause and over $V$ only in the second (see also the addition spelled out under sub-claim ii.).

    \begin{enumerate}[label=\roman*.)]
        \item \emph{No edge with an arrowhead at a $J$-node.}
              For every $j \in J$ and every $v \in J \cup V$,
              \[ j \hus v \notin G. \]
              Unfolding the shorthand $\hus$ via def \ref{not-cdmg} item~6 (so $j \hus v \in G$ abbreviates $(v, j) \in E \lor s(j, v) \in L$, with the L-disjunct expressed via the unordered pair $s(j, v) = s(v, j)$ in place of the ordered pair $(j, v)$ used pre-refactor), this is equivalent to the set-theoretic conjunction
              \[ (v, j) \notin E \;\land\; s(j, v) \notin L. \]

        \item \emph{The type of $G$ does not forbid a directed edge from a $J$-node into a $V$-node.}
              For every $j \in J$ and every $v \in V$, the ordered pair $(j, v)$ satisfies $j \in J \cup V$ and $v \in V$, so the typing constraint $E \subseteq (J \cup V) \times V$ of def \ref{def-cdmg} is compatible with $(j, v) \in E$; equivalently, no restriction in def \ref{def-cdmg} forbids the inclusion of $(j, v)$ in $E$ (in the notation of \ref{not-cdmg}: no restriction forbids $j \tuh v \in G$). This is a \emph{permission} statement, not an existence statement: $(j, v) \in E$ is admissible by the definition for every $(j, v) \in J \times V$, but is not asserted to hold.

        \item \emph{No two $J$-nodes are adjacent.}
              For every $j_1 \in J$ and every $j_2 \in J$, the nodes $j_1$ and $j_2$ are \emph{not} adjacent in $G$ in the sense of def \ref{def-edge-relations} item~i.; equivalently,
              \[ (j_1, j_2) \notin E \;\land\; (j_2, j_1) \notin E \;\land\; s(j_1, j_2) \notin L. \]
    \end{enumerate}
\end{Rem}

% --- proof ------------------------------------------------------------------
\begin{proof}
All three sub-claims rest on the same underlying tool: the typing constraints carried by def \ref{def-cdmg}. Specifically: $E \subseteq (J \cup V) \times V$ (so the second component of any pair in $E$ lies in $V$); the quotient-typing constraint on $L$, which under the refactored encoding states that for every $s \in L$ and every node $v$ appearing in $s$ (i.e.\ every endpoint of the unordered pair $s$), $v \in V$; and $J \cap V = \emptyset$. We prove each sub-claim in turn.

\medskip
\emph{Proof of (i).}
Fix $j \in J$ and $v \in J \cup V$, and suppose, for contradiction, that $j \hus v \in G$. By def \ref{not-cdmg} item~6, the shorthand $j \hus v \in G$ abbreviates the disjunction
\[ (v, j) \in E \;\lor\; s(j, v) \in L. \]
We rule out both disjuncts.
\begin{itemize}
    \item If $(v, j) \in E$, then by the typing constraint $E \subseteq (J \cup V) \times V$ of def \ref{def-cdmg}, the second component of any element of $E$ lies in $V$; hence $j \in V$. But $j \in J$ by assumption and $J \cap V = \emptyset$ (def \ref{def-cdmg}), so $j \in J \cap V = \emptyset$, a contradiction.
    \item If $s(j, v) \in L$, then by the typing constraint on $L$ from def \ref{def-cdmg}, every node appearing in any element of $L$ lies in $V$. The unordered pair $s(j, v)$ has $j$ as one of its two endpoints (it is the equivalence class of the ordered pair $(j, v)$ under the swap relation, so the multiset of its endpoints is $\{j, v\}$), so $j$ appears in $s(j, v)$ and consequently $j \in V$. The same contradiction $j \in J \cap V = \emptyset$ follows.
\end{itemize}
Both disjuncts therefore fail, so $j \hus v \notin G$. Unfolding the shorthand once more, this is the conjunction $(v, j) \notin E \land s(j, v) \notin L$, as stated.

\medskip
\emph{Proof of (ii).}
Fix $j \in J$ and $v \in V$. We must show that the typing constraint $E \subseteq (J \cup V) \times V$ of def \ref{def-cdmg} is compatible with the ordered pair $(j, v)$ -- i.e.\ that $(j, v) \in (J \cup V) \times V$, so that nothing in def \ref{def-cdmg} forbids the membership $(j, v) \in E$. The membership $(j, v) \in (J \cup V) \times V$ is, by the definition of a Cartesian product, the conjunction
\[ j \in J \cup V \;\land\; v \in V. \]
The second conjunct is the standing hypothesis $v \in V$. For the first conjunct, $j \in J$ gives $j \in J \cup V$ by inclusion of the left summand into the union. Hence $(j, v) \in (J \cup V) \times V$, which is the typing precondition for an element of $E$. No further restriction in def \ref{def-cdmg} excludes the pair $(j, v)$ from $E$ (the only other constraints concern $L$, not $E$, and the disjointness $J \cap V = \emptyset$ is compatible with $(j, v)$ since $j \in J$ and $v \in V$ are unrelated by intersection). Thus the inclusion $(j, v) \in E$ is admissible by the definition, which is the permission statement claimed. (We emphasise that this argument does not assert that $(j, v) \in E$ holds for any particular CDMG: it asserts only that the definition of CDMG does not preclude it.)

\medskip
\emph{Proof of (iii).}
Fix $j_1 \in J$ and $j_2 \in J$, and suppose, for contradiction, that $j_1$ and $j_2$ are adjacent in $G$. By def \ref{def-edge-relations} item~i., adjacency unfolds to the disjunction
\[ (j_1, j_2) \in E \;\lor\; (j_2, j_1) \in E \;\lor\; s(j_1, j_2) \in L. \]
We rule out all three disjuncts.
\begin{itemize}
    \item If $(j_1, j_2) \in E$, then $E \subseteq (J \cup V) \times V$ (def \ref{def-cdmg}) forces the second component $j_2 \in V$. But $j_2 \in J$, so $j_2 \in J \cap V = \emptyset$ (def \ref{def-cdmg}), a contradiction.
    \item If $(j_2, j_1) \in E$, the same constraint applied to the second component yields $j_1 \in V$; combined with $j_1 \in J$ this gives $j_1 \in J \cap V = \emptyset$, a contradiction.
    \item If $s(j_1, j_2) \in L$, then by the typing constraint on $L$ from def \ref{def-cdmg}, every node appearing in any element of $L$ lies in $V$. The endpoints of the unordered pair $s(j_1, j_2)$ are $j_1$ and $j_2$, so \emph{both} $j_1 \in V$ and $j_2 \in V$; combined with $j_1 \in J$ this gives $j_1 \in J \cap V = \emptyset$, a contradiction. (Either component on its own suffices; we pick $j_1$.)
\end{itemize}
All three disjuncts therefore fail, so $j_1$ and $j_2$ are not adjacent; equivalently the conjunction $(j_1, j_2) \notin E \land (j_2, j_1) \notin E \land s(j_1, j_2) \notin L$ holds, as stated.

The argument above does not separate the cases $j_1 = j_2$ and $j_1 \neq j_2$: the typing-driven contradictions apply uniformly. We note in passing that the diagonal case $j_1 = j_2 =: j$ also follows: the $E$-branches give $j \in V$ as before, and on the $L$-branch the membership $s(j, j) \in L$ would in addition violate the irreflexivity of $L$ from def \ref{def-cdmg} -- which under the refactored quotient typing forbids any diagonal class $s(v, v)$ from lying in $L$ -- on top of forcing $j \in V$. No diagonal subtlety arises.
\end{proof}

\end{document}
tex_proof.html:
Remark — CDMGRestrictions

Let $G = (J, V, E, L)$ be a CDMG (def def-cdmg); we use the notation of not-cdmg throughout. Recall, per def def-cdmg, that $E \subseteq (J \cup V) \times V$, and that $L \subseteq (V \times V) / {\sim}$ is a finite set of unordered pairs of elements of $V$, where $\sim$ is the swap relation $(v_1, v_2) \sim (v_2, v_1)$ on $V \times V$. We write $s(a, b)$ for the equivalence class of the ordered pair $(a, b)$ under $\sim$; i.e.\ $s(a, b)$ is the unordered pair $\{a, b\}$ regarded as an element of the quotient $(V \times V) / {\sim}$, and in particular $s(a, b) = s(b, a)$ holds by construction. $L$ is required to be irreflexive in the sense that no diagonal class lies in $L$: $s(v, v) \notin L$ for every $v$. No separate symmetry axiom on $L$ is needed – symmetry is built into the quotient typing. Finally, $J \cap V = \emptyset$.

The restrictions encoded in def def-cdmg have the following three consequences. In the LN wording, the clauses “$j \hus v \notin G$” and “edges $j \tuh v$ are allowed” carry an implicit universal quantification over the second-position variable $v$; we surface these quantifiers explicitly below, with $v$ ranging over $J \cup V$ in the first clause and over $V$ only in the second (see also the addition spelled out under sub-claim ii.).

  1. No edge with an arrowhead at a $J$-node. For every $j \in J$ and every $v \in J \cup V$, \[ j \hus v \notin G. \] Unfolding the shorthand $\hus$ via def not-cdmg item 6 (so $j \hus v \in G$ abbreviates $(v, j) \in E \lor s(j, v) \in L$, with the L-disjunct expressed via the unordered pair $s(j, v) = s(v, j)$ in place of the ordered pair $(j, v)$ used pre-refactor), this is equivalent to the set-theoretic conjunction \[ (v, j) \notin E \;\land\; s(j, v) \notin L. \]
  2. The type of $G$ does not forbid a directed edge from a $J$-node into a $V$-node. For every $j \in J$ and every $v \in V$, the ordered pair $(j, v)$ satisfies $j \in J \cup V$ and $v \in V$, so the typing constraint $E \subseteq (J \cup V) \times V$ of def def-cdmg is compatible with $(j, v) \in E$; equivalently, no restriction in def def-cdmg forbids the inclusion of $(j, v)$ in $E$ (in the notation of not-cdmg: no restriction forbids $j \tuh v \in G$). This is a permission statement, not an existence statement: $(j, v) \in E$ is admissible by the definition for every $(j, v) \in J \times V$, but is not asserted to hold.
  3. No two $J$-nodes are adjacent. For every $j_1 \in J$ and every $j_2 \in J$, the nodes $j_1$ and $j_2$ are not adjacent in $G$ in the sense of def def-edge-relations item i.; equivalently, \[ (j_1, j_2) \notin E \;\land\; (j_2, j_1) \notin E \;\land\; s(j_1, j_2) \notin L. \]
Proof.

All three sub-claims rest on the same underlying tool: the typing constraints carried by def def-cdmg. Specifically: $E \subseteq (J \cup V) \times V$ (so the second component of any pair in $E$ lies in $V$); the quotient-typing constraint on $L$, which under the refactored encoding states that for every $s \in L$ and every node $v$ appearing in $s$ (i.e.\ every endpoint of the unordered pair $s$), $v \in V$; and $J \cap V = \emptyset$. We prove each sub-claim in turn.

Proof of (i). Fix $j \in J$ and $v \in J \cup V$, and suppose, for contradiction, that $j \hus v \in G$. By def not-cdmg item 6, the shorthand $j \hus v \in G$ abbreviates the disjunction \[ (v, j) \in E \;\lor\; s(j, v) \in L. \] We rule out both disjuncts.

  • If $(v, j) \in E$, then by the typing constraint $E \subseteq (J \cup V) \times V$ of def def-cdmg, the second component of any element of $E$ lies in $V$; hence $j \in V$. But $j \in J$ by assumption and $J \cap V = \emptyset$ (def def-cdmg), so $j \in J \cap V = \emptyset$, a contradiction.
  • If $s(j, v) \in L$, then by the typing constraint on $L$ from def def-cdmg, every node appearing in any element of $L$ lies in $V$. The unordered pair $s(j, v)$ has $j$ as one of its two endpoints (it is the equivalence class of the ordered pair $(j, v)$ under the swap relation, so the multiset of its endpoints is $\{j, v\}$), so $j$ appears in $s(j, v)$ and consequently $j \in V$. The same contradiction $j \in J \cap V = \emptyset$ follows.

Both disjuncts therefore fail, so $j \hus v \notin G$. Unfolding the shorthand once more, this is the conjunction $(v, j) \notin E \land s(j, v) \notin L$, as stated.

Proof of (ii). Fix $j \in J$ and $v \in V$. We must show that the typing constraint $E \subseteq (J \cup V) \times V$ of def def-cdmg is compatible with the ordered pair $(j, v)$ – i.e.\ that $(j, v) \in (J \cup V) \times V$, so that nothing in def def-cdmg forbids the membership $(j, v) \in E$. The membership $(j, v) \in (J \cup V) \times V$ is, by the definition of a Cartesian product, the conjunction \[ j \in J \cup V \;\land\; v \in V. \] The second conjunct is the standing hypothesis $v \in V$. For the first conjunct, $j \in J$ gives $j \in J \cup V$ by inclusion of the left summand into the union. Hence $(j, v) \in (J \cup V) \times V$, which is the typing precondition for an element of $E$. No further restriction in def def-cdmg excludes the pair $(j, v)$ from $E$ (the only other constraints concern $L$, not $E$, and the disjointness $J \cap V = \emptyset$ is compatible with $(j, v)$ since $j \in J$ and $v \in V$ are unrelated by intersection). Thus the inclusion $(j, v) \in E$ is admissible by the definition, which is the permission statement claimed. (We emphasise that this argument does not assert that $(j, v) \in E$ holds for any particular CDMG: it asserts only that the definition of CDMG does not preclude it.)

Proof of (iii). Fix $j_1 \in J$ and $j_2 \in J$, and suppose, for contradiction, that $j_1$ and $j_2$ are adjacent in $G$. By def def-edge-relations item i., adjacency unfolds to the disjunction \[ (j_1, j_2) \in E \;\lor\; (j_2, j_1) \in E \;\lor\; s(j_1, j_2) \in L. \] We rule out all three disjuncts.

  • If $(j_1, j_2) \in E$, then $E \subseteq (J \cup V) \times V$ (def def-cdmg) forces the second component $j_2 \in V$. But $j_2 \in J$, so $j_2 \in J \cap V = \emptyset$ (def def-cdmg), a contradiction.
  • If $(j_2, j_1) \in E$, the same constraint applied to the second component yields $j_1 \in V$; combined with $j_1 \in J$ this gives $j_1 \in J \cap V = \emptyset$, a contradiction.
  • If $s(j_1, j_2) \in L$, then by the typing constraint on $L$ from def def-cdmg, every node appearing in any element of $L$ lies in $V$. The endpoints of the unordered pair $s(j_1, j_2)$ are $j_1$ and $j_2$, so both $j_1 \in V$ and $j_2 \in V$; combined with $j_1 \in J$ this gives $j_1 \in J \cap V = \emptyset$, a contradiction. (Either component on its own suffices; we pick $j_1$.)

All three disjuncts therefore fail, so $j_1$ and $j_2$ are not adjacent; equivalently the conjunction $(j_1, j_2) \notin E \land (j_2, j_1) \notin E \land s(j_1, j_2) \notin L$ holds, as stated.

The argument above does not separate the cases $j_1 = j_2$ and $j_1 \neq j_2$: the typing-driven contradictions apply uniformly. We note in passing that the diagonal case $j_1 = j_2 =: j$ also follows: the $E$-branches give $j \in V$ as before, and on the $L$-branch the membership $s(j, j) \in L$ would in addition violate the irreflexivity of $L$ from def def-cdmg – which under the refactored quotient typing forbids any diagonal class $s(v, v)$ from lying in $L$ – on top of forcing $j \in V$. No diagonal subtlety arises.

lean_blocks.explanation: This `variable` line is the chapter-wide statement context shared by every theorem in the file. Lean 4's auto-binding mechanism implicitly inserts these binders into any declaration that mentions `Node` or `DecidableEq Node`, so although the line is written once it is part of each theorem's signature. `Node : Type*` is kept fully polymorphic — fixing it would force every downstream chapter (CBNs, do-calculus, iSCMs, discovery) to renumber. The `[DecidableEq Node]` typeclass is the minimal one inherited from `def_3_1`: a `CDMG`'s `J, V : Finset Node` and `E, L : Finset (Node × Node)` require decidable equality for `Disjoint G.J G.V`, for membership tests on `G.J ∪ G.V`, and for unfolding `G.hus` (a disjunction over `Finset` membership). The wrapping marker uses three dashes (`--- helper`) rather than the two-dash `-- start statement` form to flag it as load-bearing statement infrastructure to the tex/Lean reconciliation tooling.
lean_blocks.code_annotated:
-- Section-wide statement context: auto-bound by Lean 4 into every theorem below.
-- `Node : Type*` stays polymorphic; `[DecidableEq Node]` is the minimal class for
-- `Finset` membership, `Disjoint G.J G.V`, and unfolding `G.hus` over the graph.
variable {Node : Type*} [DecidableEq Node]

lean_blocks.explanation: This `variable` line is the chapter-wide statement context shared by every theorem in the file. Lean 4's auto-binding mechanism implicitly inserts these binders into any declaration that mentions `Node` or `DecidableEq Node`, so although the line is written once it is part of each theorem's signature. `Node : Type*` is kept fully polymorphic — fixing it would force every downstream chapter (CBNs, do-calculus, iSCMs, discovery) to renumber. The `[DecidableEq Node]` typeclass is the minimal one inherited from `def_3_1`: a `CDMG`'s `J, V : Finset Node` and `E, L : Finset (Node × Node)` require decidable equality for `Disjoint G.J G.V`, for membership tests on `G.J ∪ G.V`, and for unfolding `G.hus` (a disjunction over `Finset` membership). The wrapping marker uses three dashes (`--- helper`) rather than the two-dash `-- start statement` form to flag it as load-bearing statement infrastructure to the tex/Lean reconciliation tooling.
lean_blocks.code_annotated:
-- Section-wide statement context: auto-bound by Lean 4 into every theorem below.
-- `Node : Type*` stays polymorphic; `[DecidableEq Node]` is the minimal class for
-- `Finset` membership, `Disjoint G.J G.V`, and unfolding `G.hus` over the graph.
variable {Node : Type*} [DecidableEq Node]

lean_blocks.explanation: This is clause (i) of the remark: for every $j \in G.J$ and every $v \in G.J \cup G.V$, there is no head-star edge `j \hus v` in `G`. The clause reflects the LN's verbatim statement "the nodes $j \in J$ will not have any arrowheads pointing towards them". The conclusion is stated via `G.hus`, the dot-notation form of the LN macro $\hus$ from `def_3_2` item 6; consumers wanting the unfolded form `(v, j) ∉ G.E ∧ s(j, v) ∉ G.L` reach it with one `unfold` step. The membership hypothesis `hv : v ∈ G.J ∪ G.V` pins `v` to the LN's quantifier range (every node of the graph), per the operator addition `[implicit_universal_quantifier_in_hus_clause]`. Implicit binders `{j}`, `{v}` follow Mathlib style: callers supply only the `hj`, `hv` proofs and Lean infers the witnesses. `G : CDMG Node` is the first explicit argument so dot-notation `G.no_arrowhead_into_J hj hv` reads naturally, matching the `G.hE_subset` / `G.adjacent` idiom used throughout Section 3.1.
lean_blocks.code_annotated:
-- Clause (i) of claim_3_1: no head-star edge points at any j ∈ J.
-- `G` first and explicit so callers write `G.no_arrowhead_into_J hj hv`.
theorem no_arrowhead_into_J (G : CDMG Node) {j : Node}
    -- `hj` witnesses j ∈ G.J; `j` itself is implicit, inferred from `hj`.
    (hj : j ∈ G.J) {v : Node} (hv : v ∈ G.J ∪ G.V) :
    -- Conclusion stated via the LN macro `hus` (def_3_2 item 6), 1:1 Lean form.
    ¬ G.hus j v

lean_blocks.explanation: This is clause (ii) of the remark: a *permission* claim, not an existence claim. For every $j \in G.J$ and every $v \in G.V$, the ordered pair $(j, v)$ satisfies the typing precondition $E \subseteq (J \cup V) \times V$ from `def_3_1`, so nothing in the definition forbids `(j, v) ∈ G.E`. The conclusion is rendered as the literal RHS of `hE_subset`'s implication, instantiated at `(j, v)` — `j ∈ G.J ∪ G.V ∧ v ∈ G.V`. A heavier extensibility form `∃ G', G'.E = G.E ∪ {(j, v)} ∧ <still a valid CDMG>` was rejected: it would require constructing a witness and re-discharging every typing field, whereas the canonical tex picked the lighter precondition form. The refactor from `L : Finset (Node × Node)` to `L : Finset (Sym2 Node)` does not surface here at all — this clause is entirely about `E`'s typing, and the body uses only `Finset.mem_union_left` on `hj` plus the standing `hv`. Implicit `{j}`, `{v}` and explicit `hj`, `hv` mirror the other two theorems.
lean_blocks.code_annotated:
-- Clause (ii): the typing constraint of def_3_1 permits a J→V directed edge.
-- Permission, not existence — the conclusion is the RHS of `hE_subset` at (j, v).
theorem J_to_V_edge_admissible (G : CDMG Node) {j : Node}
    -- `hj : j ∈ G.J` gives the first conjunct via `Finset.mem_union_left`.
    (hj : j ∈ G.J) {v : Node} (hv : v ∈ G.V) :
    -- Conjunction matches `hE_subset`'s conclusion shape; trivial under refactor.
    j ∈ G.J ∪ G.V ∧ v ∈ G.V

lean_blocks.explanation: This is clause (iii) of the remark: no two `J`-nodes are adjacent in `G`. The conclusion is stated via `G.adjacent`, the dot-notation form of `def_3_3` item i, matching the canonical tex's pointer "not adjacent in $G$ in the sense of def \ref{def-edge-relations} item~i.". Consumers wanting the unfolded three-disjunct shape `(j₁, j₂) ∈ G.E ∨ (j₂, j₁) ∈ G.E ∨ s(j₁, j₂) ∈ G.L` reach it with one `unfold` of `adjacent` / `sus` / `tuh` / `hut` / `huh`. No `j₁ ≠ j₂` precondition is stated: the LN says "no two nodes in $J$ are adjacent" without restricting to distinct nodes, and the diagonal case is covered uniformly — the `huh` self-loop is killed by `hL_irrefl` (now "no diagonal Sym2 class lies in `L`"), and the directed self-loop is killed by `hE_subset` forcing `j ∈ G.V`. Symmetry of `adjacent` is also unstated; under the `Sym2` refactor it is *definitional* on the L-disjunct (no `hL_symm` invocation needed), so a one-line corollary suffices. Implicit `{j₁}`, `{j₂}` follow the same convention as the other two theorems.
lean_blocks.code_annotated:
-- Clause (iii): no two J-nodes are adjacent in G; covers the diagonal j₁ = j₂.
-- `adjacent` is the dot-notation form of def_3_3 item i.
theorem J_nodes_not_adjacent (G : CDMG Node) {j₁ : Node}
    -- `hj₁`, `hj₂` are the only hypotheses; no distinctness assumption.
    (hj₁ : j₁ ∈ G.J) {j₂ : Node} (hj₂ : j₂ ∈ G.J) :
    -- Stated via the named predicate, not the unfolded `tuh ∨ hut ∨ huh`.
    ¬ G.adjacent j₁ j₂

addition_to_the_LN: [implicit_universal_quantifier_in_hus_clause] The statements "$j \hus v \notin G$" and "edges $j \tuh v$ are allowed" carry implicit universal quantification over $v$. Precisely: for every $j \in J$ and every node $v$ in the graph, $j \hus v \notin G$; and for every $j \in J$ and every $v \in V$, an edge $j \tuh v$ is permitted in $G$ (its presence is not asserted, only that no restriction forbids it).
design_choices:
**Split the remark into three independent theorems rather than a single conjunction.** The lecture notes group three consequences of the CDMG typing constraints into one numbered remark, so a tempting rendering is one theorem of the form `∀ j v, P₁ ∧ P₂ ∧ P₃`. That packaging is rejected because the three clauses are conceptually disjoint and downstream consumers want them piecewise: walks (`def_3_4`) need (i) to rule out walks *ending* at a `J`-node by a head, family relationships (`def_3_5`) combine (i) and (iii) to force `Pa^G(j) = ∅` for $j \in J$, and hard intervention (`def_3_10`) leverages (ii) when promoting `V`-nodes to `J`-nodes. A monolithic conjunction also forces every consumer to destructure, and the three clauses bind different quantifier ranges anyway — clause (i) ranges $v$ over $J \cup V$ while clause (ii) ranges $v$ over $V$ only. Three theorems mirrors the upstream style: `CDMGNotation.lean` splits its seven LN items, `EdgeRelations.lean` splits its three.

**State each clause via its named upstream relation, not the unfolded set-theoretic conjunction.** Clause (i) is rendered as `¬ G.hus j v` rather than the expansion `(v, j) ∉ G.E ∧ s(j, v) ∉ G.L`, and clause (iii) as `¬ G.adjacent j₁ j₂` rather than the three-way disjunction-of-memberships over `G.E` and `G.L`. The unfolded form is closer to the rewrite's expansion sentence, but `CDMGNotation` and `EdgeRelations` exist precisely so the LN macros $\hus$ and "adjacent" have a 1:1 Lean form; any consumer that needs the unfolded shape gets it with one `unfold CDMG.hus` (or `unfold CDMG.adjacent CDMG.sus CDMG.tuh CDMG.hut CDMG.huh`) step. Faithfulness to the LN macros wins over inlining their definitions.

**Render clause (ii) as the typing-constraint precondition `j ∈ G.J ∪ G.V ∧ v ∈ G.V`, not an extensibility statement.** Clause (ii) is a *permission* claim, and a closer reading of "permitted" suggests an extensibility witness `∃ G', G'.E = G.E ∪ {(j, v)} ∧ <still a valid CDMG>`. That form is rejected as heavier — it requires constructing a witness and re-discharging every typing field — and because the canonical tex picked the lighter precondition form: exactly the RHS of `hE_subset`'s implication, instantiated at `(j, v)`. Consumers needing the extensibility version derive it from this theorem. For the same reason, the conclusion uses set-membership rather than the $\tuh$ notation: encoding $\tuh$ would force the extensibility reading.

**Keep `Node` polymorphic with only `[DecidableEq Node]`, and wrap the `variable` line as a load-bearing `--- helper` block.** Fixing `Node` to a concrete carrier here would force every downstream chapter (CBNs, do-calculus, iSCMs, discovery) to renumber, so the chapter-wide convention is preserved. `[DecidableEq Node]` is the minimal typeclass inherited from `def_3_1` — needed for `Disjoint G.J G.V`, membership on `G.J ∪ G.V`, the unfolding of `G.hus` (a disjunction over `Finset` membership), and Mathlib's derived `DecidableEq (Sym2 Node)` instance that keeps the refactored `L : Finset (Sym2 Node)` carrier computable. The `variable` line uses three-dash `--- start helper` / `--- end helper` markers, not the two-dash `-- start statement` form, because Lean 4's auto-binding makes it part of every theorem's signature: it is load-bearing statement context, not throwaway sugar, and the helper marker flags that distinction to the tex/Lean reconciliation tooling. The whole file sits inside `namespace CDMG` to enable dot notation (`G.hus`, `G.adjacent`) that mirrors the LN's left-to-right macro reading.

**Implicit node binders, explicit membership hypotheses, and `G` first.** Each theorem makes `j`, `v` (or `j₁`, `j₂`) implicit while keeping the membership proofs `hj`, `hv` explicit — standard Mathlib style so callers supply only the hypotheses and let Lean infer the witnesses. `G : CDMG Node` is the first explicit argument so dot-notation `G.no_arrowhead_into_J hj hv` reads naturally and matches the existing `G.adjacent`, `G.hE_subset`, `G.hL_subset` idiom throughout Section 3.1.

**Omit a `j₁ ≠ j₂` precondition in clause (iii).** The LN says "no two nodes in $J$ are adjacent" without restricting to distinct nodes, and the diagonal case $j_1 = j_2$ is covered uniformly: the `huh` self-loop case `s(j, j) ∈ G.L` is killed by `hL_irrefl` (under the refactor, phrased as "no diagonal `Sym2` class lies in `L`"), and a directed self-loop `(j, j) ∈ G.E` forces $j \in G.V$ via `hE_subset`, contradicting `Disjoint G.J G.V`. Adding a distinctness hypothesis would weaken the theorem and drift away from the LN. Symmetry of `adjacent` is likewise left unstated: under the refactored `L : Finset (Sym2 Node)` it is *definitional* on the L-disjunct (no `hL_symm` invocation needed, since symmetry is built into the `Sym2` quotient), so a one-line corollary suffices.

**Port the L-branch of each proof to the `Sym2` membership idiom.** The refactor moved `L` from `Finset (Node × Node)` to `Finset (Sym2 Node)`, so `hL_subset` now reads `∀ s ∈ L, ∀ v ∈ s, v ∈ V` rather than the old ordered-pair-component form. The L-branches of `no_arrowhead_into_J` and `J_nodes_not_adjacent` therefore extract `j ∈ G.V` (resp. `j₁ ∈ G.V`) via `G.hL_subset h (Sym2.mem_mk_left j v)` — Mathlib's canonical witness that the left endpoint of `s(j, v)` is a member of the quotient class — in one step, with no `Sym2.lift` destructuring or representative choice. The E-branches port mechanically: `E : Finset (Node × Node)` was *not* retyped, and `hE_subset` is unchanged. Clause (ii) is structurally invisible to the refactor — it depends only on $E$'s typing precondition.
